source_paper_title: Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gao_2023, title={Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.161}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.161}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gao, Xuefeng and Huang, Junfei}, year={2023}, month=june }
context:
Section 1 (step 2 discussion) and Conclusion/Section 9, pages 3–4 and 54

Developing efficient algorithms for multi-stage SPs is an active area of research. Although we solve the SP for some simple examples in Section 8, solving more general cases is well beyond the scope of this paper...

How to extend this strategy to multistage SPs is an issue yet to be explored.
title: Computationally efficient methods for solving multistage stochastic programs in ATO inventory control
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-review assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system with:
1) A set of products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) and components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\) where \(a_{ji}\) units of component \(j\) are required per unit of product \(i\).
3) Deterministic component lead times taking \(K\) distinct values \(0<L_1<\cdots<L_K\). Components are grouped by lead time: \(A^k\) denotes the submatrix of \(A\) consisting of the rows (components) whose lead time equals \(L_k\), and \(h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^{n_k}\) are their holding costs.
4) Backlog costs \(b\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\). Define \(c=b+\sum_{k=1}^K (A^k)^\top h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\).
5) Demand over time is modeled via random vectors \(D^k\in\mathbb{Z}_+^m\) (\(k=1,\dots,K\)) representing demand increments over disjoint lead-time-related intervals. (Only their distributions matter for the stochastic program below.)

A lower bound on the long-run average cost is given by the optimal value \(\varphi_K\) of a \((K+1)\)-stage stochastic program:
\[
\varphi_K=\inf_{y_K\in\mathbb{R}^{n_K}}\Bigl\{h^K\cdot y_K+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{K-1}(y_K,D^K)\bigr]\Bigr\},
\]
\[
\varphi_k(y_{k+1},\dots,y_K,x)=\inf_{y_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_k}}\Bigl\{h^k\cdot y_k+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{k-1}(y_k,\dots,y_K,x+D^k)\bigr]\Bigr\},\quad k=1,\dots,K-1,
\]
and the last-stage linear program
\[
\varphi_0(y_1,\dots,y_K,x)=-\max_{z\in\mathbb{R}^m}\Bigl\{c\cdot z\ \big|\ z\le x,\ A^k z\le y_k\ (k=1,\dots,K)\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m\) represents accumulated demand over the entire longest lead-time window, and the decisions \(y_k\) represent (relaxed) component-availability quantities at each stage.

The paper emphasizes that to implement their asymptotically optimal policy, one must repeatedly (re-)solve such multistage stochastic programs (or high-dimensional LP approximations of them) as system state changes. However, scenario-tree formulations can have extremely high dimension when \(K\) is large and/or lead times are long, making direct solution computationally burdensome.
problem_definition_latex: Design algorithmic methods that, for general \(K\), general bill-of-materials matrix \(A\), and general (given) demand distributions of \((D^1,\dots,D^K)\), can solve (exactly or to a controllable accuracy) the multistage stochastic program defining \(\varphi_K\) above with substantially reduced computational complexity relative to the naive scenario-tree linear-program formulation, in a way suitable for repeated re-optimization over time within an ATO control policy.
latex:
Consider a continuous-review assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system with:
1) A set of products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) and components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\) where \(a_{ji}\) units of component \(j\) are required per unit of product \(i\).
3) Deterministic component lead times taking \(K\) distinct values \(0<L_1<\cdots<L_K\). Components are grouped by lead time: \(A^k\) denotes the submatrix of \(A\) consisting of the rows (components) whose lead time equals \(L_k\), and \(h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^{n_k}\) are their holding costs.
4) Backlog costs \(b\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\). Define \(c=b+\sum_{k=1}^K (A^k)^\top h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\).
5) Demand over time is modeled via random vectors \(D^k\in\mathbb{Z}_+^m\) (\(k=1,\dots,K\)) representing demand increments over disjoint lead-time-related intervals. (Only their distributions matter for the stochastic program below.)

A lower bound on the long-run average cost is given by the optimal value \(\varphi_K\) of a \((K+1)\)-stage stochastic program:
\[
\varphi_K=\inf_{y_K\in\mathbb{R}^{n_K}}\Bigl\{h^K\cdot y_K+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{K-1}(y_K,D^K)\bigr]\Bigr\},
\]
\[
\varphi_k(y_{k+1},\dots,y_K,x)=\inf_{y_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_k}}\Bigl\{h^k\cdot y_k+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{k-1}(y_k,\dots,y_K,x+D^k)\bigr]\Bigr\},\quad k=1,\dots,K-1,
\]
and the last-stage linear program
\[
\varphi_0(y_1,\dots,y_K,x)=-\max_{z\in\mathbb{R}^m}\Bigl\{c\cdot z\ \big|\ z\le x,\ A^k z\le y_k\ (k=1,\dots,K)\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m\) represents accumulated demand over the entire longest lead-time window, and the decisions \(y_k\) represent (relaxed) component-availability quantities at each stage.

The paper emphasizes that to implement their asymptotically optimal policy, one must repeatedly (re-)solve such multistage stochastic programs (or high-dimensional LP approximations of them) as system state changes. However, scenario-tree formulations can have extremely high dimension when \(K\) is large and/or lead times are long, making direct solution computationally burdensome.

Design algorithmic methods that, for general \(K\), general bill-of-materials matrix \(A\), and general (given) demand distributions of \((D^1,\dots,D^K)\), can solve (exactly or to a controllable accuracy) the multistage stochastic program defining \(\varphi_K\) above with substantially reduced computational complexity relative to the naive scenario-tree linear-program formulation, in a way suitable for repeated re-optimization over time within an ATO control policy.
location_in_paper: Section 1 (step 2 discussion) and Conclusion/Section 9, pages 3–4 and 54
open_problem_quote:
Developing efficient algorithms for multi-stage SPs is an active area of research. Although we solve the SP for some simple examples in Section 8, solving more general cases is well beyond the scope of this paper...

How to extend this strategy to multistage SPs is an issue yet to be explored.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is an algorithm-design problem rather than a conjectured inequality or structural claim; there is no clear finite instance space where a single counterexample would resolve the question.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms (decomposition, approximations, rolling-horizon schemes) can be represented as code and evaluated on simulated ATO instances by runtime/accuracy trade-offs, but the objective is not crisply specified as a single computable fitness across all instances, and theoretical guarantees would still be needed.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The multistage stochastic program \(\varphi_K\) in Reiman–Wan–Wang (2021) arises as a transformed version of the earlier lower-bound program \(\inf_{\alpha\ge 0}\{b\cdot\alpha+\Phi_K(\alpha)\}+b\cdot\mathbb{E}[\bar D]\) of Reiman–Wang (2012). The 2021 paper shows how to remove the problematic infimum over \(\alpha\) by shifting variables, yielding a finite optimal solution to \(\varphi_K\) and enabling policy parameters (inventory-position and backlog targets) to be recomputed as the state changes. It also proposes a finite-support truncation \(D^k\wedge M\) leading to a finite-dimensional scenario-tree LP approximation \(\varphi_{K,M}\), and proves uniform approximation plus Lipschitz stability of optimal solutions with constants independent of the scenario-tree size. This stability is crucial for their stochastic tracking arguments and diffusion-scale asymptotic optimality.

From the algorithmic perspective, prior ATO work on two-stage SPs (Dogru–Reiman–Wang 2010; Dogru–Reiman–Wang 2017) demonstrates that substantial computational gains are possible by exploiting BOM structure (e.g., W and M systems) to derive decompositions and region-based characterizations of optimal allocations. Complementary computational work aimed at industrial-scale ATO optimization (van Jaarsveld–Scheller-Wolf 2015) and structure identification (Zipkin 2016) suggests that scalable formulations often hinge on recognizing special constraint patterns and separability. On the general multistage SP side, the standard stochastic programming toolbox (Shapiro–Dentcheva–Ruszczyński 2009) provides decomposition and sampling-based methods (nested Benders, SDDP) that are natural candidates to replace naive scenario-tree LPs when \(K\) is large and re-optimization is frequent.

Overall, there is no definitive, generally accepted computationally efficient (polynomial-time or provably scalable) algorithm specialized to the general ATO multistage program \(\varphi_K\) with arbitrary BOM \(A\) and general demand distributions; the problem remains open as stated. The most promising directions combine (i) distributional approximation/truncation with error control (as in \(\varphi_{K,M}\)), (ii) decomposition across stages and/or components using dual variables and BOM-induced sparsity, and (iii) reuse/warm-start across repeated solves in time, potentially via cutting-plane value-function approximations (SDDP-style) tailored to the last-stage LP \(\varphi_0\) and its dual structure.

sources.title: A stochastic program based lower bound for assemble-to-order inventory systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the multistage stochastic-program lower bound \(\inf_{\alpha\ge 0}\{b\cdot\alpha+\Phi_K(\alpha)\}+b\cdot\mathbb{E}[\bar D]\) for continuous-review ATO systems and proves it is a lower bound on the long-run average cost.
sources.connection: This is the precursor SP formulation; computationally efficient methods for repeatedly solving the transformed SP \(\varphi_K\) build on this lower-bound construction and its scenario-tree structure.

sources.title: Asymptotically optimal inventory control for assemble-to-order systems with identical lead times
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops an SP-based policy and proves diffusion-scale asymptotic optimality for ATO systems with identical lead times, using a two-stage SP and tracking/backlog-target ideas.
sources.connection: Motivates repeated solution of (multi)stage SPs for control; highlights that computational tractability is crucial even in the two-stage case, and suggests structural exploitation and approximations.

sources.title: A stochastic programming based inventory policy for assemble-to-order systems with application to the W model
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies a two-stage stochastic program for the W-system and derives an implementable SP-based inventory policy; demonstrates performance and discusses structural properties enabling computation in special BOMs.
sources.connection: Provides early evidence that exploiting BOM structure can make SPs solvable in special cases; suggests decomposition/structure-aware algorithms as a path to computational efficiency.

sources.title: Assemble-to-order inventory management via stochastic programming: Chained BOMs and the M-system
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes SP formulations for chained BOM (M-system), deriving structural results and computational approaches tailored to that BOM; connects optimal allocation regions to SP solutions.
sources.connection: Demonstrates that BOM-induced structure can reduce SP complexity; offers templates for extending structure-exploiting algorithms beyond two-stage to multistage settings.

sources.title: Optimization of industrial-scale assemble-to-order systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops computational optimization methods for large ATO systems, emphasizing scalable formulations and algorithmic techniques suitable for industrial-scale instances.
sources.connection: Directly relevant to computational efficiency for ATO optimization; while not targeting the exact multistage SP \(\varphi_K\), it provides scalable algorithmic ideas (decomposition/approximation) that can be adapted for repeated re-optimization.

sources.title: Some specially structured assemble-to-order systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Identifies special ATO structures that admit simplifications and sharper analysis, often enabling reduced-dimensional optimization or closed-form characterizations.
sources.connection: Highlights structural regimes where the multistage SP may simplify (fewer effective stages/constraints), guiding algorithm design that exploits BOM/lead-time structure.

sources.title: A primal-dual framework for decomposition and rounding methods in ATO systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes a primal-dual decomposition/rounding framework for ATO optimization problems, aiming at scalable computation and implementable integer solutions.
sources.connection: Suggests decomposition strategies that could be integrated with multistage SP solution (e.g., stagewise decomposition, Lagrangian relaxation) to reduce scenario-tree complexity and support repeated re-optimization.

sources.title: Lectures on Stochastic Programming: Modeling and Theory
sources.theorem_or_result: Comprehensive theory of multistage stochastic programming, including scenario-tree formulations, decomposition methods (e.g., nested Benders/SDDP), and convergence guarantees.
sources.connection: Provides the main algorithmic toolbox (SDDP, decomposition, sampling-based approximations) for solving multistage SPs like \(\varphi_K\) more efficiently than naive scenario trees, especially under repeated re-optimization.
